chunks.text: The 2018 Distinguished EECS Alumni awards were presented to Prof. Marie desJardins, Prof. Andrea Goldsmith, Richard Ruby, and EECS Prof. Emeritus Eric Brewer, at the Berkeley EECS Annual Research Symposium (BEARS) on February 8, 2018. CS alumna Marie desJardins (Ph.D. ’92), currently a Professor of Computer Science at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County, was recognized for her contributions to artificial intelligence and computer science education. Prof. Andrea Goldsmith, a professor at Stanford University, was honored for her work in wireless communications. Richard Ruby, an industry leader, and Prof. Emeritus Eric Brewer, known for his research in distributed systems, also received awards for their achievements in the field.

Alumna Dr. Estela Llinás (MEng ’68/Ph.D. ’77) graduated from Berkeley with a Ph.D. in EECS 40 years ago. As one of an elite group of women during her era, she reflects on her childhood, her experiences as a woman engineer, and her role as a pioneer in the field. Her journey highlights the challenges and achievements of women in engineering, contributing to the historical narrative of diversity and inclusion in EECS at Berkeley.

In 2016, research in the EECS department achieved several firsts, building on the momentum from 2015 when Ali Javey's team created the first optoelectronically perfect monolayer semiconductor. The year 2016 saw groundbreaking advancements in various areas, including new technologies and innovations that pushed the boundaries of electrical engineering and computer science, marking it as a significant period of progress and discovery within the department.
